patient_record.content:
History and Physical Note
Patient: David Thompson
DOB: 03/14/1972
Gender: Male
Phone: +91-98450-11223
Address: 17 MG Road, Indiranagar, Bangalore
Visit Date: 01/10/2024
Subjective Notes:
David, a 51-year-old male, presents for follow-up of Type 2 Diabetes. Reports increased thirst and
urination over the past week. No chest pain or shortness of breath. Diet mostly vegetarian; exercises
3x a week. Sleep and mood are normal.
Objective Notes:
Vitals: BP 140/88, Pulse 82, Temp 98.7°F. Physical exam unremarkable. No signs of foot ulcers.
Normal heart and lung sounds.
Assessment Notes:
Diagnosis: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (E11.9)
Plan Notes:
Increase metformin dosage to 1000mg BID. Order HbA1c, Lipid Profile. Schedule nutritionist
follow-up. Return in 3 months.

History and Physical Note
Patient: Anjali Mehra
DOB: 08/25/1990
Gender: Female
Phone: +91-98180-11245
Address: 202 Lakeview Apartments, Pune
Visit Date: 03/15/2024
Subjective Notes:
Anjali presents with 5-day history of dry cough and mild fever. Denies shortness of breath. Took
paracetamol with partial relief.
Objective Notes:
Vitals: BP 118/76, Pulse 96, Temp 100.2°F. Mild pharyngeal erythema. Chest auscultation clear.
Assessment Notes:
Diagnosis: Upper Respiratory Infection (J06.9)
Plan Notes:
Symptomatic management with antihistamines and fluids. Rest advised. Follow-up in 5 days if no
improvement.

History and Physical Note
Patient: Ramesh Kulkarni
DOB: 11/02/1958
Gender: Male
Phone: +91-98220-45322
Address: 52 Residency Road, Chennai
Visit Date: 02/28/2024
Subjective Notes:
Ramesh presents for routine checkup. No current complaints. History of hypertension. Regular on
medication.
Objective Notes:
Vitals: BP 130/84, Pulse 76, Temp 98.4°F. Cardiovascular and respiratory exams normal. No
peripheral edema.
Assessment Notes:
Diagnosis: Essential Hypertension (I10)
Plan Notes:
Continue Telmisartan 40mg OD. Recommend lifestyle modifications. Routine labs ordered. Return
in 6 months.

history.query: “My 70-year-old father has chronic kidney disease. I want to book a nephrologist for him. Also, can you summarize latest treatment methods?”

medical_documents.content:
Disease: Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD)

Overview:
Chronic Kidney Disease is a gradual loss of kidney function over time. It can progress to kidney failure if left untreated.

Symptoms:
- Fatigue
- Swelling in the legs and ankles
- High blood pressure
- Loss of appetite
- Nausea
- Decreased urine output
- Muscle cramps

Risk Factors:
- Diabetes
- Hypertension
- Family history of kidney disease
- Smoking
- Obesity

Diagnosis:
- Blood creatinine test
- eGFR
- Urine protein test
- Kidney ultrasound

Treatment:
- Blood pressure control
- ACE inhibitors or ARBs
- Blood sugar management
- Low protein diet
- Dialysis in advanced stages
- Kidney transplantation

Lifestyle Recommendations:
- Reduce salt intake
- Exercise regularly
- Stay hydrated
- Avoid smoking

Disease: Hypertension

Overview:
Hypertension is persistently elevated blood pressure that increases the risk of cardiovascular disease.

Symptoms:
- Often no symptoms
- Headaches
- Dizziness
- Blurred vision
- Chest pain (severe cases)
- Shortness of breath

Risk Factors:
- High salt diet
- Obesity
- Smoking
- Alcohol consumption
- Family history

Diagnosis:
- Blood pressure measurement
- Ambulatory blood pressure monitoring

Treatment:
- ACE inhibitors
- Calcium channel blockers
- Diuretics
- Beta blockers

Lifestyle Recommendations:
- Reduce salt intake
- Exercise regularly
- Lose excess weight
- Stop smoking

Disease: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus

Overview:
Type 2 Diabetes is a chronic disease in which the body becomes resistant to insulin or does not produce enough insulin.

Symptoms:
- Increased thirst
- Frequent urination
- Increased hunger
- Fatigue
- Blurred vision
- Slow wound healing
- Weight changes

Risk Factors:
- Obesity
- Sedentary lifestyle
- Family history
- Age over 45
- Hypertension

Diagnosis:
- Fasting blood glucose
- HbA1c
- Oral glucose tolerance test

Treatment:
- Lifestyle modification
- Metformin
- GLP-1 receptor agonists
- Insulin when required

Lifestyle Recommendations:
- Regular exercise
- Healthy diet
- Weight management
- Blood sugar monitoring

external_reference.title: Chronic Kidney Disease
external_reference.summary: You have two kidneys, each about the size of your fist.  Their main job is to filter your blood. They remove wastes and extra water, which become urine. They also keep the body's chemicals balanced, help control blood pressure, and make hormones.Chronic kidney disease (CKD) means that your kidneys are damaged and can't filter blood as they should. This damage can cause wastes to build up in your body. It can also cause other problems that can harm your health. Diabetes and high blood pressure are the most common causes of CKD.The kidney damage occurs slowly over many years. Many people don't have any symptoms until their kidney disease is very advanced. Blood and urine tests are the only way to know if you have kidney disease.Treatments cannot cure kidney disease, but they may slow kidney disease. They include medicines to lower blood pressure, control blood sugar, and lower cholesterol. CKD may still get worse over time. Sometimes it can lead to kidney failure. If your kidneys fail, you will need  dialysis or a kidney transplantation.You can take steps to keep your kidneys healthier longer:Choose foods with less  salt (sodium)Control your blood pressure; your health care provider can tell you what your blood pressure should beKeep your blood sugar in the target range, if you have diabetesLimit the amount of alcohol you drinkChoose foods that are healthy for your heart: fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and low-fat dairy foodsLose weight if you are overweightBe physically activeDon't smokeNIH: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases
